title: Why I'm Building 30 Insurance AI Apps in 30 Days
hooks: I spent 19 years watching insurance teams drown in PDFs.
So I set myself a challenge: build 30 working AI tools for the London Market in 30 days.
cta: What workflow would you want an AI tool to handle first — intake triage, document review, or exposure monitoring?

title: The Architecture That Makes 30 Apps Feel Like One System
hooks: Most AI demos die because they were built to impress, not to maintain.
When I designed the architecture for 30 insurance AI apps, I started with one question: what would make app number 30 as fast to build as app number 3?
cta: If you were reviewing this architecture for a production insurance team, where would you pressure-test it first?

title: The Nerdy Version: How Every Request Flows Through the Stack
hooks: This one is for the engineers.
I am building 30 insurance AI apps in 30 days and shipping every one publicly. Here is exactly how the data moves.
cta: Comment "nerdy" if you want me to walk through a specific layer.

title: The Roadmap: 30 Apps Grouped by Insurance Workflow
hooks: I did not pick 30 random AI use cases.
I mapped every app to a specific insurance workflow, weighted by where I have seen the most operational friction.
cta: Which bucket would create the most value for your team this quarter?

title: Day Zero: The Most Important App I Never Shipped
hooks: Before I built a single feature, I spent a full day building nothing visible.
Day Zero was about answering one question: when I am on Day 22, will the platform still make it easy to ship?
cta: If you are building at pace, start with the operating system, not the first feature. What does your Day Zero look like?

title: Day 1: From Broker Submission to Triage Decision in Minutes
hooks: An underwriter I worked with once told me: "I spend 70% of my time on submissions I'll decline."
Day 1 of my 30-day challenge tackles this head-on.
cta: If you were building this for an underwriting team, what field would you want surfaced first?

title: Section Start: Lloyd's and Specialty Underwriting
hooks: The best underwriting teams I have worked with share one trait.
They are not the ones with the most data. They are the ones who reach the right decision fastest.
cta: Where does your underwriting flow lose the most time today?

title: Day 2: Portfolio Concentration Should Be Visible Before It Becomes a Loss
hooks: Good portfolios fail quietly.
Concentration builds in the wrong class, the wrong territory — and nobody notices until the loss event reveals the pattern.
cta: Would you use this as a daily monitoring tool or a pre-bind decision aid?

title: Day 3: Your Risk Appetite Document Is Probably Useless to Your Systems
hooks: Every syndicate has a risk appetite statement. Most of them are PDFs that sit in SharePoint.
Day 3 converts policy text into actionable underwriting rules.
cta: How do you manage appetite drift today — manually, with a rules layer, or not at all?

title: Day 4: A Lloyd's Slip Can Hide the Issue in Plain Sight
hooks: If you have ever reviewed a Market Reform Contract slip, you know the feeling.
Forty pages. Somewhere in there is the clause that matters.
cta: What clause type would you auto-highlight first in a real market review?

title: Day 5: When the Class Code Is Wrong, Everything Downstream Breaks
hooks: "Professional indemnity for a software consultancy providing AI advisory services to financial institutions."
What class of business is that? PI? Technology E&O? Financial institutions?
cta: Would you trust this as an underwriter assistant or a front-door validation gate?

title: Section Wrap: Five Underwriting Tools — What I Learned
hooks: Five tools. Five underwriting bottlenecks. One week.
The hardest problems are not technical. They are domain problems.
cta: If you improved one lever in underwriting this quarter, would it be appetite clarity, triage speed, or portfolio visibility?

title: Section Start: Exposure Management — If You Cannot See It, You Cannot Control It
hooks: I once sat in a board meeting where someone asked: "What is our total insured value within 50 miles of the Florida coastline?"
The room went quiet.
cta: Where is your biggest exposure blind spot today?

title: Day 6: The Spreadsheet Said $200M Exposure. The Map Said "All in One Postcode."
hooks: This is a real pattern I have seen.
Spreadsheet totals hide spatial concentration. Maps reveal it.
cta: What would make this production-ready first for your team?

title: Day 7: When a Cat Event Breaks, Your Team Needs a Briefing — Not a News Feed
hooks: Hurricane makes landfall. Earthquake in Turkey. Flooding across Northern Europe.
Within hours, every reinsurer is scrambling for the same information.
cta: Should a cat briefing tool optimise for speed, depth, or strict one-page format?

title: Section Wrap: Exposure Management — From Spreadsheets to Spatial Intelligence
hooks: Two tools. Two perspectives on the same problem.
One is proactive surveillance. The other is reactive response.
cta: Which metric should drive weekly exposure action first?

title: Section Start: Claims Ops — Where Process Meets Promise
hooks: The moment a policyholder files a claim is the moment your product is actually tested.
Everything before that is a promise. Claims is where the promise gets kept or broken.
cta: What is the first claims handoff you would improve without reducing control?

title: Day 8: The Renewal Looked Identical Until I Diffed the Endorsement
hooks: Renewal season. The slip comes back marked "as expiring." The broker says nothing has changed.
Then six months later, a claim is declined because a sub-limit was halved.
cta: Where would this save the most time in your organisation?

title: Day 9: Your Referral Queue Is Lying to You About Priority
hooks: "Work the queue in order."
A $500K PI referral gets the same queue position as a $15M construction liability with three coverage flags.
cta: Would you replace queue order entirely or use scored priority as reviewer guidance?

title: Day 10: The First Notice of Loss Is Where Speed and Accuracy Collide
hooks: A claims handler opens their inbox on Monday morning. Fourteen new FNOLs.
Without triage, all fourteen get the same treatment.
cta: If you could automate one claims handoff tomorrow without reducing control, which one would it be?

title: Day 11: A Reserve Number Without Reasoning Is Just a Guess with Authority
hooks: "Set initial reserve at $750K."
Every claims handler has written a sentence like this. But the real question is: why $750K?
cta: Should initial reserve suggestions come with confidence bands or point estimates?

title: Section Wrap: Claims Ops — Four Tools Across the Claims Lifecycle
hooks: Endorsement diff. Referral scoring. FNOL triage. Reserve reasoning.
Four tools. One design principle: enhance the handler's judgment, do not replace it.
cta: What is your lead KPI in claims: cycle time, leakage reduction, or consistency of triage outcomes?

title: What Broke and What I Fixed by Day 3
hooks: Three days in. Three apps shipped. And a growing list of things that nearly derailed each one.
Building at pace is a stress test for your infrastructure.
cta: What guardrail gave your team the highest leverage early in a build cycle?

title: Ten Days In: The System Is Compounding
hooks: Day 1 took an entire weekend.
Day 10 shipped in under four hours — including tests, deployment, database setup, and visual QA.
cta: If you want speed without chaos, standardise the hard parts early. What did you standardise?

title: Ten Lessons from Ten Days: Speed Comes from Standards, Not Shortcuts
hooks: I shipped 11 insurance AI apps in 10 days.
Here is what actually worked, what did not, and what I would tell someone starting a similar challenge.
cta: Which of these resonates most with how your team builds?

title: Section Start: Broking and Placement — The Right Risk Still Needs the Right Market Path
hooks: A broker once told me the hardest part of placement is not finding a market.
It is telling the right story to the right market at the right time.
cta: Where do placements stall most for your team?

title: Section Wrap: Broking and Placement — Preparation Wins Markets
hooks: The best placement outcomes I have seen never came from the fastest broker.
They came from the best-prepared broker.
cta: What would lift placement outcomes more: better risk narratives or cleaner market execution?

title: Section Start: Productivity — Remove Friction, Not Judgment
hooks: Every insurance team I have worked with has the same problem disguised as ten different symptoms.
These are not ten problems. They are one problem: too much manual work between receiving information and acting on it.
cta: Which repetitive task in your team should be removed first?

title: Section Wrap: Productivity — The Gains Come from Removing Friction
hooks: The biggest productivity improvements I have seen in insurance did not come from new software.
They came from removing one unnecessary handoff.
cta: Which workflow should be standardised next to remove avoidable rework?

title: Section Start: Leadership — The Operating Rhythm Is the Strategy
hooks: I have worked with leaders who had brilliant strategies and teams that could not execute them.
The problem was never talent. It was operating rhythm.
cta: Which operating rhythm would improve outcomes fastest?

title: Section Wrap: Leadership Tools — Systems Over Slogans
hooks: The best leaders I have worked with did not have the most charismatic presence.
They had the clearest operating rhythms.
cta: Which operating rhythm would improve outcomes fastest?

title: Section Start: Compliance — The Best Controls Do Not Slow the Business
hooks: Compliance gets a bad reputation in insurance.
Because the controls were bolted on after the workflow was designed, not built into it.
cta: Which control could be redesigned from a separate process into an integrated workflow step?

title: Section Wrap: Compliance — Built In, Not Bolted On
hooks: Three tools. Three compliance workflows. One design principle.
The control should be invisible to the user.
cta: Which compliance control could be redesigned from a separate process into an integrated workflow step?
